A commit adding `feature_a.py`.\n2. A commit modifying `feature_a.py` and adding `feature_b.py`.\n3. A commit fixing a typo in `feature_a.py`.\n\nYour task is to rewrite the history to make it a clean series of two commits:\n1. The first commit should contain all changes related to `feature_a.py` (i.e., squash the typo fix into the first commit, and split out the `feature_a.py` modifications from the second commit into the first commit).\n2. The second commit should contain only the addition of `feature_b.py`.\n\nAfter rewriting the history, move the `feature-stack` bookmark to point to the new second commit.\nFinally, rebase the entire two-commit stack onto the `main` bookmark.\n\nTo verify your work, run `jj log -r 'main..feature-stack' -T 'description ++ \"\\n\"' > /home/user/workspace/final_log.txt`. help"},"output":{"output":"Jujutsu (An experimental VCS)\r\n\r\nTo get started, see the tutorial [`jj help -k tutorial`].\r\n\r\n[`jj help -k tutorial`]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/tutorial/\r\n\r\nUsage: jj [OPTIONS] [COMMAND]\r\n\r\nCommands:\r\n  abandon           Abandon a revision\r\n  absorb            Move changes from a revision into the stack of mutable revisions\r\n  bisect            Find a bad revision by bisection\r\n  bookmark          Manage bookmarks [default alias: b]\r\n  commit            Update the description and create a new change on top [default alias: ci]\r\n  config            Manage config options\r\n  describe          Update the change description or other metadata [default alias: desc]\r\n  diff              Compare file contents between two revisions\r\n  diffedit          Touch up the content changes in a revision with a diff editor\r\n  duplicate         Create new changes with the same content as existing ones\r\n  edit              Sets the specified revision as the working-copy revision\r\n  evolog            Show how a change has evolved over time [aliases: evolution-log]\r\n  file              File operations\r\n  fix               Update files with formatting fixes or other changes\r\n  gerrit            Interact with Gerrit Code Review\r\n  git               Commands for working with Git remotes and the underlying Git repo\r\n  help              Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)\r\n  interdiff         Show differences between the diffs of two revisions\r\n  log               Show revision history\r\n  metaedit          Modify the metadata of a revision without changing its content\r\n  new               Create a new, empty change and (by default) edit it in the working copy\r\n  next              Move the working-copy commit to the child revision\r\n  operation         Commands for working with the operation log [aliases: op]\r\n  parallelize       Parallelize revisions by making them siblings\r\n  prev              Change the working copy revision relative to the parent revision\r\n  rebase            Move revisions to different parent(s)\r\n  redo              Redo the most recently undone operation\r\n  resolve           Resolve conflicted files with an external merge tool\r\n  restore           Restore paths from another revision\r\n  revert            Apply the reverse of the given revision(s)\r\n  root              Show the current workspace root directory (shortcut for `jj workspace root`)\r\n  show              Show commit description and changes in a revision\r\n  sign              Cryptographically sign a revision\r\n  simplify-parents  Simplify parent edges for the specified revision(s)\r\n  sparse            Manage which paths from the working-copy commit are present in the working copy\r\n  split             Split a revision in two\r\n  squash            Move changes from a revision into another revision\r\n  status            Show high-level repo status [default alias: st]\r\n  tag               Manage tags\r\n  undo              Undo the last operation\r\n  unsign            Drop a cryptographic signature\r\n  util              Infrequently used commands such as for generating shell completions\r\n  version           Display version information\r\n  workspace         Commands for working with workspaces\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\n  -V, --version\r\n          Print version\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. My goal is to pinpoint the exact usage parameters to achieve the intended result.\n\n\n","state":"done"},{"type":"tool-executeCommand","toolCallId":"fHSmhnAZ6GVNDyXP","state":"output-available","input":{"command":"cd /home/user/workspace/repo && jj squash --help"},"output":{"output":"Move changes from a revision into another revision\r\n\r\nWithout any options, moves the changes from the working-copy revision to the parent revision.\r\n\r\nWith the `-r` option, moves the changes from the specified revision to the parent revision. Fails if\r\nthere are several parent revisions (i.e., the given revision is a merge).\r\n\r\nWith the `--from` and/or `--into` options, moves changes from/to the given revisions. If either is\r\nleft out, it defaults to the working-copy commit. For example, `jj squash --into @--` moves changes\r\nfrom the working-copy commit to the grandparent.\r\n\r\nIf, after moving changes out, the source revision is empty compared to its parent(s), and\r\n`--keep-emptied` is not set, it will be abandoned. Without `--interactive` or paths, the source\r\nrevision will always be empty.\r\n\r\nIf the source was abandoned and both the source and destination had a non-empty description, you\r\nwill be asked for the combined description. If either was empty, then the other one will be used.\r\n\r\nIf a working-copy commit gets abandoned, it will be given a new, empty commit. This is true in\r\ngeneral; it is not specific to this command.\r\n\r\nThe name \"squash\" comes from the idea of combining (squashing) the changes from multiple revisions\r\ntogether.\r\n\r\nEXPERIMENTAL FEATURES\r\n\r\nAn alternative squashing UI is available via the `-o`, `-A`, and `-B` options. Using any of these\r\noptions creates a new commit. They can be used together with one or more `--from` options (if no\r\n`--from` is specified, `--from @` is assumed).\r\n\r\nUsage: jj squash [OPTIONS] [FILESETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [FILESETS]...\r\n          Move only changes to these paths (instead of all paths)\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -r, --revision <REVSET>\r\n          Revision to squash into its parent (default: @). Incompatible with the experimental\r\n          `-o`/`-A`/`-B` options\r\n\r\n  -f, --from <REVSETS>\r\n          Revision(s) to squash from (default: @)\r\n\r\n  -t, --into <REVSET>\r\n          Revision to squash into (default: @)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --to]\r\n\r\n  -o, --onto <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to use as parent for the new commit (can be repeated to\r\n          create a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: -d, --destination]\r\n\r\n  -A, --insert-after <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to insert the new commit after (can be repeated to create a\r\n          merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --after]\r\n\r\n  -B, --insert-before <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to insert the new commit before (can be repeated to create\r\n          a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --before]\r\n\r\n  -m, --message <MESSAGE>\r\n          The description to use for squashed revision (don't open editor)\r\n\r\n  -u, --use-destination-message\r\n          Use the description of the destination revision and discard the description(s) of the\r\n          source revision(s)\r\n\r\n      --editor\r\n          Open an editor to edit the change description\r\n          \r\n          Forces an editor to open when using `--message` to allow the message to be edited\r\n          afterwards.\r\n\r\n  -i, --interactive\r\n          Interactively choose which parts to squash\r\n\r\n      --tool <NAME>\r\n          Specify diff editor to be used (implies --interactive)\r\n\r\n  -k, --keep-emptied\r\n          The source revision will not be abandoned\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). By default,\r\nthe output only includes mutable revisions, along with some additional revisions for context. Use\r\n`jj log -r ::` to see all revisions. See [`jj help -k revsets`] for information about the syntax.\r\n\r\n[`jj help -k revsets`]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/revsets/\r\n\r\nSpans of revisions that are not included in the graph per `--revisions` are rendered as a synthetic\r\nnode labeled \"(elided revisions)\".\r\n\r\nThe working-copy commit is indicated by a `@` symbol in the graph. [Immutable revisions] have a `◆`\r\nsymbol. Other commits have a `○` symbol. All of these symbols can be [customized].\r\n\r\n[Immutable revisions]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/config/#set-of-immutable-commits\r\n\r\n[customized]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/config/#node-style\r\n\r\nUsage: jj log [OPTIONS] [FILESETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [FILESETS]...\r\n          Show revisions modifying the given paths\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -r, --revisions <REVSETS>\r\n          Which revisions to show\r\n          \r\n          If no paths nor revisions are specified, this defaults to the `revsets.log` setting.\r\n\r\n  -n, --limit <LIMIT>\r\n          Limit number of revisions to show\r\n          \r\n          Applied after revisions are filtered and reordered topologically, but before being\r\n          reversed.\r\n\r\n      --reversed\r\n          Show revisions in the opposite order (older revisions first)\r\n\r\n  -G, --no-graph\r\n          Don't show the graph, show a flat list of revisions\r\n\r\n  -T, --template <TEMPLATE>\r\n          Render each revision using the given template\r\n          \r\n          Run `jj log -T` to list the built-in templates.\r\n          \r\n          You can also specify arbitrary template expressions using the [built-in keywords]. See\r\n          [`jj help -k templates`] for more information.\r\n          \r\n          If not specified, this defaults to the `templates.log` setting.\r\n          \r\n          [built-in keywords]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/templates/#commit-keywords\r\n          \r\n          [`jj help -k templates`]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/templates/\r\n\r\n  -p, --patch\r\n          Show patch\r\n\r\n      --count\r\n          Print the number of commits instead of showing them\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nDiff Formatting Options:\r\n  -s, --summary\r\n          For each path, show only whether it was modified, added, or deleted\r\n\r\n      --stat\r\n          Show a histogram of the changes\r\n\r\n      --types\r\n          For each path, show only its type before and after\r\n          \r\n          The diff is shown as two letters. The first letter indicates the type before and the\r\n          second letter indicates the type after. '-' indicates that the path was not present, 'F'\r\n          represents a regular file, `L' represents a symlink, 'C' represents a conflict, and 'G'\r\n          represents a Git submodule.\r\n\r\n      --name-only\r\n          For each path, show only its path\r\n          \r\n          Typically useful for shell commands like: `jj diff -r @- --name-only | xargs perl -pi\r\n          -e's/OLD/NEW/g`\r\n\r\n      --git\r\n          Show a Git-format diff\r\n\r\n      --color-words\r\n          Show a word-level diff with changes indicated only by color\r\n\r\n      --tool <TOOL>\r\n          Generate diff by external command\r\n          \r\n          A builtin format can also be specified as `:<name>`. For example, `--tool=:git` is\r\n          equivalent to `--git`.\r\n\r\n      --context <CONTEXT>\r\n          Number of lines of context to show\r\n\r\n      --ignore-all-space\r\n          Ignore whitespace when comparing lines\r\n\r\n      --ignore-space-change\r\n          Ignore changes in amount of whitespace when comparing lines\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML\r\n          expression.
